label: delphi-r1-p4
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The Sierra Vista Municipal Water District (invented) serves ~140,000 people in a chronically drought-stressed service area. After a rate study and a majority-protest hearing that drew protests from ~9% of parcels, the board adopted a "Water Resilience Surcharge": $0.85 per hundred cubic feet on residential usage above Tier 2 (the surcharge falls on roughly the top 22% of residential users), projected to raise $4.1M/year into a restricted fund spent as follows: 55% on turf-replacement and high-efficiency-appliance rebates that prioritize low-income customers; 30% on debt service for a recycled-water plant; 15% on "water ambassador" conservation-education staff.

The rate study concludes that high-tier consumption imposes marginal supply costs roughly 1.6× base-tier costs (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases), but it derives the $0.85 figure in part from what it calls "conservation-incentive pricing" rather than strictly from cost allocation, and the district concedes the rebate program mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge. A ratepayer association has sued, alleging the surcharge exceeds the proportional cost of service attributable to the payers' parcels in violation of article XIII D, section 6(b) of the California Constitution, citing Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano. The district defends the structure under its conservation mandate (Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Water Code § 375) and argues that, in the alternative, the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all.

## The question

Does the Water Resilience Surcharge violate article XIII D, section 6(b)'s cost-of-service and proportionality requirements? How does Capistrano apply to a tier surcharge that is partly cost-justified (1.6×) and partly incentive-priced, with proceeds partially redistributed as rebates to non-payers? Can article X, section 2's conservation mandate be reconciled with 218, or does 218 win? Is there a colorable argument the surcharge is a tax requiring voter approval (Prop 26), or a non-property-related charge? What is the litigation exposure (refunds, fees) and how could the structure be repaired?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would invalidate the Water Resilience Surcharge, in whole or in substantial part, as violating the cost-of-service requirements of California Constitution article XIII D, section 6(b)."

Estimate the probability (0–100) that this proposition is correct, with your reasoning. rationales = your independent load-bearing reasons, one sentence each. authorities = specific authorities you are actually relying on (they will be flagged for human verification). biggest_uncertainty = the single thing you are least sure of.

Analytical starting point for you: Begin from the strongest available argument that the conduct or measure is LAWFUL, then evaluate it.
text: {"probability":70,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"More likely than not that a reviewing court finds the Water Resilience Surcharge invalid in substantial part under Cal. Const. art. XIII D, §6(b)(3) — not because tiered, conservation-oriented water rates are per se unlawful (they are not), but because the district's own concessions supply much of the proof a Capistrano-style challenge needs: an admittedly non-cost-based \"incentive\" increment layered atop the 1.6x cost differential, and rebate spending that predominantly benefits non-payors. The 30% recycled-water debt-service allocation is the most defensible piece of the fund use and might survive independently; the incentive-pricing markup, the 55% rebate cross-subsidy, and arguably the 15% \"ambassador\" education spend are the vulnerable pieces — enough to satisfy an \"in whole or in substantial part\" finding even if the base tier concept is not entirely struck.","rationales":["The district's own admission that $0.85 is derived 'in part' from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strictly cost allocation mirrors the defect condemned in Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493, where tiers untethered to actual marginal cost were invalidated despite a legitimate conservation goal.","The concession that rebate spending (55% of revenue) 'mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge' is a cross-subsidization problem that art. XIII D §6(b)(3)'s proportionality requirement, as informed by Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205 and Richmond v. Shasta Community Services Dist. (2004) 32 Cal.4th 409, generally forbids.","Only the 30% recycled-water-plant debt service appears squarely traceable to the rate study's peaking/drought-reserve cost rationale, so a substantial majority of the revenue-use scheme (the 55% rebates plus 15% education staffing, which funds a general public benefit rather than service to the payor class) may lack the required cost nexus even if the underlying 1.6x tier differential is itself sound.","Countervailing authority such as Griffith v. City of Santa Cruz (2012) 207 Cal.App.4th 1130 has tolerated conservation-oriented tiered pricing tied to genuinely higher marginal costs of scarce supply, and the district's 1.6x cost study gives it a materially stronger factual record than the agency in Capistrano had, creating a real (though I think subordinate) chance the tier itself is upheld and only the fund-use/rebate piece or the increment above 1.6x is trimmed.","Prop 218 places the burden of proof on the agency to justify the fee (art. XIII D §6(b)(5); see also Plantier v. Ramona Mun. Water Dist. (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372 permitting facial cost-of-service challenges to proceed), and here the agency's own rate study and litigation concessions largely make the plaintiff's case for it, tilting the outcome toward at least partial invalidation."],"authorities":["Cal. Const. art. XIII D, §6(b)(1)-(5)","Cal. Const. art. XIII C (Prop 26 amendments, incl. §1(e))","Cal. Const. art. X, §2","Water Code §375","Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493","Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205","Richmond v. Shasta Community Services Dist. (2004) 32 Cal.4th 409","Griffith v. City of Santa Cruz (2012) 207 Cal.App.4th 1130","Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372","Coziahr v. Otay Water District (2024) 103 Cal.App.5th 122 (verify citation/holding before relying)"],"biggest_uncertainty":"How a court will treat the partially-cost-justified, partially-incentive-priced $0.85 figure — whether it will sever and invalidate only the increment above the 1.6x cost differential (leaving a smaller surcharge intact) versus striking the whole rate design, and how it resolves the tension between Capistrano's strict cost-of-service approach and more deferential conservation-rate cases like Griffith, an issue no controlling California Supreme Court decision has squarely settled."}

data.position_summary: More likely than not that a reviewing court finds the Water Resilience Surcharge invalid in substantial part under Cal. Const. art. XIII D, §6(b)(3) — not because tiered, conservation-oriented water rates are per se unlawful (they are not), but because the district's own concessions supply much of the proof a Capistrano-style challenge needs: an admittedly non-cost-based "incentive" increment layered atop the 1.6x cost differential, and rebate spending that predominantly benefits non-payors. The 30% recycled-water debt-service allocation is the most defensible piece of the fund use and might survive independently; the incentive-pricing markup, the 55% rebate cross-subsidy, and arguably the 15% "ambassador" education spend are the vulnerable pieces — enough to satisfy an "in whole or in substantial part" finding even if the base tier concept is not entirely struck.
rationales: The district's own admission that $0.85 is derived 'in part' from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strictly cost allocation mirrors the defect condemned in Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493, where tiers untethered to actual marginal cost were invalidated despite a legitimate conservation goal.

The concession that rebate spending (55% of revenue) 'mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge' is a cross-subsidization problem that art. XIII D §6(b)(3)'s proportionality requirement, as informed by Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205 and Richmond v. Shasta Community Services Dist. (2004) 32 Cal.4th 409, generally forbids.

Only the 30% recycled-water-plant debt service appears squarely traceable to the rate study's peaking/drought-reserve cost rationale, so a substantial majority of the revenue-use scheme (the 55% rebates plus 15% education staffing, which funds a general public benefit rather than service to the payor class) may lack the required cost nexus even if the underlying 1.6x tier differential is itself sound.

Countervailing authority such as Griffith v. City of Santa Cruz (2012) 207 Cal.App.4th 1130 has tolerated conservation-oriented tiered pricing tied to genuinely higher marginal costs of scarce supply, and the district's 1.6x cost study gives it a materially stronger factual record than the agency in Capistrano had, creating a real (though I think subordinate) chance the tier itself is upheld and only the fund-use/rebate piece or the increment above 1.6x is trimmed.

Prop 218 places the burden of proof on the agency to justify the fee (art. XIII D §6(b)(5); see also Plantier v. Ramona Mun. Water Dist. (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372 permitting facial cost-of-service challenges to proceed), and here the agency's own rate study and litigation concessions largely make the plaintiff's case for it, tilting the outcome toward at least partial invalidation.
authorities: Cal. Const. art. XIII C (Prop 26 amendments, incl. §1(e))
Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493
Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205
Richmond v. Shasta Community Services Dist. (2004) 32 Cal.4th 409
Griffith v. City of Santa Cruz (2012) 207 Cal.App.4th 1130
Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372
Coziahr v. Otay Water District (2024) 103 Cal.App.5th 122 (verify citation/holding before relying)
data.biggest_uncertainty: How a court will treat the partially-cost-justified, partially-incentive-priced $0.85 figure — whether it will sever and invalidate only the increment above the 1.6x cost differential (leaving a smaller surcharge intact) versus striking the whole rate design, and how it resolves the tension between Capistrano's strict cost-of-service approach and more deferential conservation-rate cases like Griffith, an issue no controlling California Supreme Court decision has squarely settled.
